headline: Penan Community Maps 200,000 Hectares of Ancestral Forest
context: Penan communities in Baram, Sarawak completed a 3-year participatory mapping project documenting 200,000 hectares of customary forest, but the maps have no legal recognition.

cards.big: The Penan mapped 200,000 hectares of ancestral forest using GPS and oral histories.
cards.sub: The Sarawak Land and Survey Department does not recognise participatory mapping as evidence of customary rights.

cards.big: 34 logging and plantation concessions overlap the mapped Penan territory.
cards.sub: Concessions were granted without consulting communities whose presence predates the concession system by centuries.

cards.big: The High Court recognised Penan customary land in 2 cases since 2015.
cards.sub: Neither ruling has been implemented. Survey and demarcation have not been conducted.

cards.big: Community-managed forests show 73% less deforestation than state concessions.
cards.sub: Global evidence from 42 countries confirms indigenous management outperforms state management on deforestation metrics.

cards.big: The community that protects the forest cannot prove it owns the forest. The company that destroys it has a licence.
Recognising community mapping as legal evidence would cost the state nothing and protect forests demonstrably better than the concession system. The obstacle is political, not legal.
